text: In this video, we are presenting a case of laparoscopic left hemicolectomy with fluorescence angiography in a patient with intestinal malrotation. Incidentally, a type II intestinal malrotation is diagnosed by CT scan in a sixty-three years old woman patient with acute diverticulitis of the sigmoid colon.

During the laparoscopic left hemicolectomy, indocyanine green fluorescence angiography is applied, highlighting the vascular supply. The abdominal x-ray reveals the presence of intestinal malrotation.

The patient is positioned supine with legs open on the operating table. Pneumoperitoneum is established using a varus needle in the left hypochondrium. The operating table is adjusted to the Trendelenburg position with the patient's right side tilted down. Four trochars are inserted for the laparoscopic procedure.

The surgeon identifies the intestinal malrotation, with the entire colon positioned on the left and the small bowel on the right. The initial surgical step involves dissecting the adhesions between the left and right colon.
The surgeon opens the mesosigma and mobilizes the sigmoid colon. The sigmoid and upper rectal arteries are identified and divided using ligature.

The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon by opening the peritocolic groove, avoiding mobilization of the splenic flexure. Dissection continues towards the recto-sigma junction, where the sigmoid colon is divided using a linear stapler.

The surgeon removes the distal part of the specimen from the abdominal cavity through a fundamental incision. Fluorescence angiographies reveal a non-vascularized colon segment approximately twenty centimeters long, necessitating a change in the previously marked section line. The new section line is marked based on the fluorescence angiography results.

Following the division of the inferior mesenteric artery and vein, the surgeon transducts the colon along the line marked by fluorescence angiography. The dissection continues up to the splenic angle to prevent tension, with fluorescence angiography confirming complete vascularization of the remaining colon.

The surgeon performs an end-to-end anastomosis using a circular stapler, joining the two ends of the colon. Following the anastomosis, a fluorescence angiography is conducted to assess the vascularization of the newly connected colon segment.
On postoperative day one, a self-limiting occult random failure was observed, but anastomotic leakage did not occur. Postoperative hospital stay was eight days.
